title: Behold the Cascades of Evidence Swelling with a Murmur!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy. Clear OCR corruption in numerals, names, punctuation, and word breaks has been repaired; differing acreage figures are preserved where the letter itself reports them. Allegations and prosecution claims remain attributed. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: As the Bengaluru disproportionate-assets trial approaches its final stage, Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh has spent four days compiling the testimony concerning assets accumulated by the accused. Because Tamil Nadu's newspapers are concealing these details, I set them out here. You have the indispensable duty of carrying them to the voters directly or through leaflets. I give them to you in the confidence that you will do so tomorrow itself.
With no further way to delay the case, Bhavani Singh has begun his argument in the Bengaluru court, presenting evidence concerning properties acquired by Jayalalithaa's side.
He read testimony concerning 898 acres at Kodanad, 1,197 acres in Tirunelveli, 200 acres at Walajabad, 100 acres at Uthukottai and twenty-five acres at Siruthavur said to belong to Jayalalithaa.

Charges were framed against Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi on 21 October 1997, and 259 witnesses were listed. After the change of government in Tamil Nadu in May 2001 and the first accused became Chief Minister, seventy-six witnesses already examined in Chennai were recalled between November 2002 and 21 February 2003. Under Section 313 of the Criminal Procedure Code, instead of requiring the accused to appear, questions were taken to their homes and statements recorded there.
DMK General Secretary Anbazhagan petitioned the Supreme Court to transfer the case to another state. The Court accepted the petition and transferred it to Bengaluru on 18 November 2003.

The case divides assets into those held before 1 July 1991 and those acquired while Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister from 1 July 1991 to 30 April 1996. Assets before the check period were valued at Rs 2,01,82,953; assets after it at Rs 64,42,89,616.

Documents from numerous companies were filed to show connections among Accused Nos. 1 to 4. The prosecution's case is that all four conspired and, using the first accused's authority as Chief Minister, accumulated assets disproportionate to income for herself and the other three.

The first accused adopted the third accused, V.N. Sudhakaran, as her foster son and conducted a magnificent wedding; evidence was filed. The prosecutor also read documents concerning companies acquired by the accused and immovable properties purchased in those companies' names.

In particular, he read testimony alleging that Sudhakaran intimidated composer Gangai Amaran into selling a twenty-two-acre property and bungalow at Siruthavur near Chennai. It is noteworthy that the Chief Minister now resides in a bungalow there.

The prosecutor cited Agriculture Department officer Radhakrishnan's testimony that he was summoned to develop Jayalalithaa's 898 acres at Kodanad. He also cited Radhakrishnan's evidence that Sudhakaran improperly sent him to Tirunelveli for private purposes, where he inspected 1,190 acres before their purchase.
He brought to the Court's attention land broker Rajaram's testimony concerning his approach to owners and purchase of 100 acres at Walajabad.

After presenting testimony on properties acquired by the accused, Bhavani Singh argued: “There is not the slightest relationship between the accused's financial position and the value of the lands accumulated. The evidence clearly shows these were purchased from disproportionate assets.”
He said key witnesses, including government officers Rajagopal and Radhakrishnan, were summoned to Poes Garden and given orders there.

Sub-Registrar Rajagopal was allegedly used to buy government lands in several places and register them under companies including Meadow Agro Farm and Lex Properties. Lands were registered in Sudhakaran's and Ilavarasi's names, and witnesses testified with evidence that purchases occurred under Sasikala's direct supervision.
Bhavani Singh gave the following property list:

1. 100 acres at Walajabad near Chennai. 2. A bungalow on twenty-five acres at Siruthavur. 3. Two acres at Neelankarai. 4. 898 acres and bungalows at Kodanad. The letter estimates one acre there at Rs 5 crore, placing the total above Rs 4,000 crore. 5. 200 acres at Kanchipuram. 6. 1,190 acres around Meenankulam, Sivarankulam and Vellankulam in Kanyakumari. 7. 200 acres in the Srivaikuntam area of Thoothukudi district. 8. 100 acres in the name of Riverway Agro Farm. 9. Cars and trucks in thirty colours. 10. A grape garden in Hyderabad.
The properties recorded through testimony over four days were valued as follows:

1. The 100 acres at Walajabad: government value Rs 40 lakh per acre and market value Rs 50 lakh, totalling Rs 40 crore and Rs 50 crore. 2. The 25.4 acres at Siruthavur: government value Rs 1.7 crore per acre and market value Rs 2 crore, totalling Rs 42.5 crore and Rs 50 crore. 3. Two acres at Neelankarai: government value Rs 35 crore and market value Rs 50 crore per acre, totalling Rs 70 crore and Rs 100 crore. 4. The 200 acres at Kanchipuram: government value Rs 30 lakh and market value Rs 50 lakh per acre, totalling Rs 60 crore and Rs 100 crore. 5. The 1,167 acres in the Srivaikuntam area: government value Rs 15 lakh and market value Rs 25 lakh per acre, totalling Rs 175 crore and Rs 292 crore. 6. Five acres at Paiyanur: government value Rs 2 crore and market value Rs 3 crore per acre, totalling Rs 10 crore and Rs 15 crore. 7. The 898 acres at Kodanad: government value Rs 3 crore and market value Rs 5 crore per acre, totalling Rs 2,450 crore and Rs 4,500 crore.

Thus, for this portion of the properties acquired while Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister from 1991 to 1996, the present government value was Rs 2,847.5 crore and market value Rs 5,107 crore. Jewellery, diamonds, company investments and movable assets would add many more crores.

Further courtroom details included testimony from a real-estate operator named Siva. He said he obtained general powers of attorney in his own name over 1,167 acres belonging to farmers in villages including Serakulam, Pandarapuram and Vallikulam in Srivaikuntam taluk, then conveyed them at a Chennai Sub-Registrar's office to River View Agro Farm.
Who owned River View Agro Farm? Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi.

The Srivaikuntam Sub-Registrar testified that he was asked to register the lands at a low valuation and with reduced stamp duty. When he refused, a Sub-Registrar from Chennai allegedly told him it was a matter involving higher authorities and pressed him to comply. He still refused. Powers of attorney over the remaining 1,153 acres were then prepared and registered in the Chennai office under Rajagopal.

While presenting evidence, Bhavani Singh referred briefly to the building beside the Poes Garden residence, purchased in the name of Jayalalithaa's mother's trust, Sandhya Natya Kala Niketan. He did not say Jayalalithaa was its sole trustee. Judge Cunha himself asked whether she was the sole trustee and had therefore signed for the purchase; Bhavani Singh replied yes.

The 898-acre Kodanad tea estate in the Nilgiris belonged to Englishman Craig Jones, who sought to sell it. Addison's and Essar came forward. The letter's account says that, after this became known, a group took the estate manager away in an unnumbered car and threatened that the estate must be sold only to a particular Chennai family.

Craig Jones complained to police on 23 October 1995, but the district Superintendent of Police allegedly intimidated him into withdrawing it. The estate, then worth Rs 10 crore, was registered in the Udaiyar family's name for Rs 7.5 crore. Within months it was registered in Sasikala's, Sudhakaran's and Ilavarasi's names. Bhavani Singh presented the corresponding testimony.
A Canara Bank officer testified that Jayalalithaa deposited Rs 1 crore and Sasikala Rs 25 lakh for three years in 1992.

A former Registrar of Companies gave detailed testimony on when and in whose names Jaya Publications, Sasi Enterprises, Lex Properties, River View Agro Farm, Signora Business, Meadow Agro Farm, Bean Beach, Ramaraj Agro Farm, Super Duper TV, Indo Chemicals and Anjaneya Printers were registered; their chairpersons, managing directors and shareholders; and their businesses. Bhavani Singh read it to the Court. A senior Tamil Nadu IAS officer also testified.

He presented evidence that lands bought from farmers were registered in the names of companies in which the accused were shareholders. Witnesses said 426.62 acres were registered from several people in the name of River View Agro on 21 and 22 December 1992.
Jayalalithaa received a salary of one rupee a month for two years as Chief Minister, a total of Rs 24, but constructed buildings worth Rs 14 crore.

Vasudevan, appointed by the Tamil Nadu government to value gold and diamond jewellery seized from Jayalalithaa's and Sasikala's homes, testified that 23 kilograms and 113 grams of gold jewellery were seized at No. 36, Poes Garden, valued at Rs 3,35,50,043.

Bhavani Singh also referred to Ebel Leelavathi's testimony that companies including Namasivaya Housing, Ayyappa Properties, Navasakthi Construction, Oceanic Construction, Lex Properties and Green Garden Apartments were registered.

Udanpirappē, these details strip away the pretence surrounding the kind of leadership under which Tamil Nadu is governed. They speak as though they have never heard the word “corruption,” call themselves spotless and self-radiant, declare every other political party corrupt, and throw dust upon us.

In the coming days of trial, we shall see what answer they give to these charges. They sought adjournment after adjournment for years so that these facts would not become known to the world. The time has come to put an end to that. Some may be deceived for many days and many for a few days, but can everyone be deceived forever?
With affection,
M.K.
31 March 2014
